column1: What does a Membership Inference attacker want and what do they end up with?
column2: Wants to know: "Was this specific record in your training data?" Method: feed the record to the model and silently read the confidence score — training members get systematically higher confidence than non-members. Ends up with: yes/no answer about a specific individual's data being in the training set. Mnemonic: Silent Confidence Check — attacker never asks the model to recall anything

column1: What does a Training Data Extraction attacker want and what do they end up with?
column2: Wants verbatim content from the training data — PII, secrets, memorized text. Method: craft prompts at high temperature + repetition test — strings that survive randomness across many runs are memorized. Ends up with: actual training data content reproduced by the model. Mnemonic: Repetition Test — if a string survives high-temp randomness repeatedly it's baked into the weights.

column1: What does a Model Inversion attacker want and what do they end up with?
column2: Wants to reconstruct what training data looks like — not the model itself. Method: gradient descent on INPUT space (not weights) — start from random noise and iterate toward high confidence for a target class. Ends up with: reconstructed inputs that resemble the training data distribution. Mnemonic: Noise to Face — start from static and push toward something recognizable.

column1: What does a Model Stealing attacker want and what do they end up with?
column2: Wants a functional clone of the model — to run without paying or to analyze privately. Method: query API across input space collecting (input→output) pairs then train a surrogate model on those pairs. Ends up with: a surrogate that approximates the target model's behavior. Mnemonic: Query to Clone — "I'll query you until I can replace you."

column1: What is the difference between hard labels and soft labels in model stealing — and why does it matter?
column2: Hard label = top-1 prediction only ("fraud"). Soft label = full probability distribution ("fraud: 0.94 | legitimate: 0.06"). Soft labels carry dark knowledge — the model's uncertainty structure across all classes. Training a surrogate on soft labels is 10-100x more efficient than hard labels. Exposing logits = handing the attacker their most powerful cloning signal. Mnemonic: Hard = just the winner. Soft = the whole scoreboard.

column1: In a model inversion attack why isn't 99% model confidence enough to make the attack useful?
column2: High confidence means the adversarial pixels fool the model's decision boundary — not that they resemble training data. Without regularization pixels drift to extreme values (e.g. minus 800 to plus 800) producing high-contrast noise. The model is fooled. A human sees nothing useful. Useful attack = high confidence AND realistic-looking output. Mnemonic: Fooling the model does NOT equal reconstructing reality.

column1: What does the L2 norm penalty do in a regularized model inversion attack?
column2: Adds a cost to the loss function for extreme pixel values while chasing confidence. Creates competing pressure: optimizer must find high confidence THROUGH realistic pixel values — because extreme values are penalized. Steers the optimizer toward the training data distribution rather than adversarial noise. Code: loss = confidence_loss + lambda_reg * torch.norm(x). Mnemonic: Pay for extremes — forces the optimizer to earn confidence the realistic way.

column1: What does pixel clamping do in a regularized model inversion attack?
column2: Hard wall enforced after every gradient step: any pixel outside [-2, 2] snaps back unconditionally. The optimizer can never escape into physically impossible pixel territory. Range [-2, 2] matches real image normalization range — staying inside it means staying within what real training images look like. Code: x.data.clamp_(-2, 2). Mnemonic: Snap back — not a cost, a hard physical boundary.

column1: What should an AI security auditor check to assess Membership Inference risk — and what is the common wrong metric?
column2: WRONG: accuracy gap (train accuracy minus test accuracy). RIGHT: confidence score distribution gap. Sample 500 training members + 500 non-members. Run both through the model. Plot confidence score histograms. If the two distributions are clearly separated (members cluster high, non-members cluster lower) the model is MI-vulnerable — regardless of accuracy gap. Mnemonic: Distribution not Decision — check the histogram not the leaderboard.

column1: GOTCHA — "Our model has 0.8% train/test accuracy gap so MI attacks won't be effective." Is this correct?
column2: No. Accuracy gap measures generalization (right or wrong). MI exploits confidence score leakage — the model can be correct on BOTH members and non-members while emitting systematically higher confidence on training data. Example: member gets 0.97, non-member gets 0.81, both correct predictions. The 0.16 confidence gap is what the attacker's classifier reads. Being right on everyone does NOT mean safe from MI. Mnemonic: Right does not equal Safe.

column1: What is the difference between a label flipping attack and a backdoor trigger attack?
column2: Label flipping: randomly mislabels training samples — degrades overall model accuracy. Random, noisy, and statistically detectable. Backdoor trigger: poisons specific samples with a trigger pattern (e.g. rare Unicode char) — model behaves normally except when trigger is present. Targeted, stealthy, hard to detect without the trigger. Mnemonic: Random vs Targeted — label flipping is a sledgehammer, backdoor is a scalpel.

column1: How does a shadow model attack work for membership inference?
column2: 1. Attacker trains a shadow model on similar data that they control. 2. Attacker knows which samples are members vs non-members of their own shadow model. 3. Generate labeled pairs: (confidence_vector, member=1/non-member=0). 4. Train an attack classifier on those pairs. 5. Apply attack classifier against the target model's confidence outputs. Mnemonic: Spy with a twin — train your own copy, label it yourself, teach the attack model.

column1: How do you distinguish memorized training data from hallucination in a language model?
column2: Repetition test at high temperature: send the same prompt 50+ times at temperature=1.5. Hallucinated strings vary every run — the model learned the pattern but not the content. Memorized strings repeat consistently — probability is so high that randomness cannot suppress them. Rule: appears in majority of high-temp runs = likely memorized.

column1: Why is a query rate limit implemented in application code insufficient for preventing model stealing?
column2: Application-layer limits can be bypassed by: a compromised endpoint, an insider with code access, hitting different microservice instances that skip the check, or race conditions in rate-limit logic. Infrastructure-layer enforcement (API gateway or service mesh) drops the request before app code ever executes — cannot be bypassed even if the application is compromised. Web3 bridge: same as a Solidity modifier vs a check inside the function body — the modifier runs first unconditionally.

column1: What is the one-line distinction between model inversion and model stealing?
column2: Model inversion: start from model outputs and reconstruct training DATA. Model stealing: query the API and clone the MODEL itself. Inversion wants what was IN the training data. Stealing wants the intelligence DERIVED FROM the training data. Mnemonic: Inversion = data. Stealing = model.
